data.title: XMMO: Using A Momentum Strategy For Midcaps To Outperform The Market
data.description:
Introduction

Nowadays, where SPDR® S&P 500® ETF Trust (SPY) trades at very high multiples, many people are searching for cheaper alternatives. While some investors opt for companies outside the U.S., many seek stocks with a lower market capitalization.

In the following article, I will explain why it makes sense to invest in midcaps and why utilizing a momentum strategy via Invesco S&P MidCap Momentum ETF (NYSEARCA:XMMO) might be a good idea.

ETF Strategy and Portfolio Analysis

XMMO runs a momentum strategy on the S&P 400 Mid-Cap index. A momentum strategy means buying stocks that have performed well over the past with the hopes that they continue to do well in the future. In XMMO, the strategy is used by selecting the 80 best-performing S&P 400 Mid-Cap index stocks.

In contrast to SPY, which has more than 33% of its exposure in technology companies, XXMO has only 7% of its exposure in this sector. Nevertheless, it has big concentrations in 3 big sectors, namely industrials (27.34%), financials (27.18%), and consumer cyclical (18.57%). All other sectors have an exposure of less than 10%. Additionally, no single stock has an allocation of more than 5%, with Williams-Sonoma (WSM) being the biggest one at 4.94%. Since the ETF only holds 80 positions at a time, one can say that overweighting some stocks to that extent does not damage the diversification effect. Also, it makes sense for a momentum ETF to have the biggest positions in the stocks that have the highest momentum, as this means a higher expected return.

Costs and Peer Group Comparison

XMMO is, overall, a very cheap ETF, with an expense ratio of only 0.34% vs. an industry average of 0.49%. Further on, the ETF bid/ask spread is at only 0.06% far cheaper than the average of 0.14%. This gives the ETF the quant rating B in the expense category here on Seeking Alpha.

Also, the ETF outperforms other mid-cap momentum ETFs like Invesco S&P MidCap Value with Momentum ETF (XMVM) and growth options like Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth Index Fund ETF Shares (VOT) on a 5-year basis by a wide margin of more than 20%.

The Case for U.S. Small Caps

One of the main reasons for U.S. small caps is the valuation. While large caps currently trade on very high multiples, mid and small caps trade on much lower multiples, as can be seen in Figure 1. Additionally, mid and small caps trade at around the same multiples, but small caps showed both lower returns and a higher drawdown in the past as can be seen in Figure 2, showing the performance ratios of small caps, mid-caps (via MDY) and large caps (via SPY).

Meanwhile, the U.S. is still a great country to invest in as it shows solid growth and is the current world power.

The Reason Momentum is Especially Beneficial for Mid-caps

In their paper “What Works on European Markets”, Phillip Vanstraceele and Tim Du Toit, test different ratios on European markets between 1999 and 2011 to find out what works. One result of this is Figure 3 and Figure 4.

As can be seen, midcaps in the three highest quintiles performed best for both a 6-month and 12-month index. Also, they outperform large caps, which is especially great since we have just seen in Figure 2 that the drawdown of mid-caps has been about the same as that of large caps.

XMMO does exactly what was described in the study, picking the top 80 out of 400 companies which would equal Q1 in Figure 3 and Figure 4.

Although the study was conducted in the European markets, it should also have implications for American stocks, since both markets follow the same structure and are strongly correlated. That momentum also works on the American markets can also be proven by the ETF's history itself. In Figure 5, one can find the performance of XMMO vs. the S&P MidCap 400 Index. As can be seen, XMMO performed on every time frame using the momentum strategy.

Figure 6 also shows the ETF's long-term performance against a normal mid-cap ETF and SPY. XMMO outperformed both ETFs and showed a higher Sharpe ratio than the normal mid-cap ETF.

Risks

While momentum strategies give higher returns, they are also slightly riskier. Although XMMO did not have a bigger drawdown than SPY and a normal mid-cap ETF, it has a higher volatility that makes the strategy less suitable for conservative investors.

Additionally, there is a small concentration risk due to the three big sector exposures. While this might be a concern, the historical volatility and drawdown show that it does not have a very big effect. However, if one sector dominates the investment landscape, concentration risk could increase as the ETF would buy more of the better-performing stocks from that sector. Because of this, it makes sense to monitor the asset allocation of the ETF while holding it.

Conclusion

Using a momentum strategy in mid-caps is a great possibility for investors thinking the S&P 500 is too expensive at the moment. Using the momentum strategy, investors can outperform both normal mid- and large-cap indices to get better risk-adjusted returns than a normal mid-cap strategy would achieve.

data.title: Vestas Wind Systems: A Buying Opportunity Amid Market Pessimism
data.description:
Investment thesis

Vestas Wind Systems (OTCPK:VWSYF) (OTCPK:VWDRY) has ended 2024 with a very strong operating performance, delivering €6.1 billion in revenue and more than tripling its EBIT before special items to €759 million in Q4 2024, significantly surpassing market expectations. Despite its improved operational performance, Vestas' stock has suffered a sharp decline, losing nearly 50% of its market capitalization over the past year. Investor sentiment has turned overly pessimistic, especially following the US election. With shares now trading at the lower end of their historical valuation range and market expectations failing to price in any potential upside from continued operational strength, we believe this presents an attractive buying opportunity.

2024 results and 2025 outlook

Vestas Wind Systems A/S reported strong financial results for the full year 2024, with revenues reaching €17.3 billion, an increase from €15.38 billion in 2023. The company also significantly improved its operating profitability, with EBIT before special items rising to €741 million, compared to €231 million in the prior year. The EBIT margin for 2024 stood at 4.3%, up from 1.5% in 2023, reflecting operational efficiency improvements and higher profitability in its order backlog.

The fourth quarter of 2024 was particularly robust, with Vestas generating €6.1 billion in revenue and an EBIT margin before special items of 12.9% that helped drive the full-year improvement. Vestas has given a double-digit margin target for its business during its investor day in 2021, and it is encouraging to see that the business is returning to a level of profitability it achieved prior 2019.

With an operating profit before special items of €759 million, Vestas surpassed market expectations of €672 million. The company also reported strong free cash flow of EUR 1.1 billion for the full year, which, we assume, has given management the confidence to reinstate a dividend and introduce a share buyback program.

Despite these strong results, Vestas’ shares faced initial selling pressure on the day of the earnings announcement, dropping more than 10% intraday. The decline appeared to be driven by the company’s 2025 EBIT margin guidance of 4-7%, which fell short of market expectations. However, during the investor call, Vestas explained that the lower EBIT guidance was primarily due to the ramp-up of production for its new V236 offshore turbine. This clarification reassured investors, leading to a recovery in the stock price. By the end of the trading day, Vestas shares closed 2.8% higher.

Legacy backlog and political headwinds weighed on stock price

In recent years, VWSYF has faced significant challenges, impacting its financial performance and weighing on the stock price. A primary concern has been the execution of legacy contracts in the Power Solutions Business signed prior to the COVID-19 pandemic. These agreements proved not to be suitable for the subsequent supply chain disruptions and inflationary pressures that emerged during the pandemic era. As a result, fulfilling these contracts over the past few years has adversely affected Vestas' profitability.

In 2019, the company reported an EBIT margin of approximately 8.27%. However, as the pandemic's effects took hold, this margin began to decline, hitting its low point at about -11% in 2022.

In 2023, Vestas managed to return to profitability, although just barely, achieving an EBIT margin before special items of 1.5%. However, it seemed that the company was on its way back to restore profitability fully as it hit another speed bump in 2024. Due to cost overruns, it had to take a write-down in its services business in the second quarter of 2024. EBIT before special items stood at a negative €185 million, resulting in a margin of -5.6%. The market did not take this lightly, as it undermined trust in the management's margin targets for the year.

Following Donald Trump's election victory in November 2024, the renewable energy sector in general and Vestas in particular took another beating. Trump's known opposition to wind energy and his commitment to eliminating offshore wind projects in the U.S. have raised concerns about potential adverse effects on Vestas' operations in the American market. In response to these developments, Vestas' share price experienced a sharp decline, reflecting investor apprehension about the company's future prospects in the U.S. As of this writing, VWSYF share price has declined by more than 47% within the last 12 months.

Business performance decoupled from share price development

Strong order backlog and restored profitability in the Power Solutions Business

Despite market pessimism following recent election results, one has to acknowledge that Vestas has delivered a robust operational performance in the last quarter, defying the sharp decline in its stock valuation.

The company reported a strong order intake of €6.5 billion in Q4 2024, pushing its total backlog to a €31.6 billion. With total orders reaching 6.5 GW at an average selling price of €1.18 million per MW in Q4 2024, Vestas anticipates revenues of €7.7 billion from these contracts — a meaningful number representing 38% of its total 2024 revenues.

The company has come a long way in restoring its profitability after suffering double-digit negative EBIT margins in 2022. In Q4 2024, Vestas achieved its long-term goal of a double-digit EBIT margin, reporting a 12.9% margin for the quarter. This is noteworthy given the milestone was reached even though the company was still executing the last of its low-margin legacy projects levels and the smaller offshore business was weighing on profitability.

Average selling prices trending up

Investors have long been wary of price wars in the wind turbine industry, where manufacturers have historically competed aggressively for market share. However, concerns over a lack of pricing discipline should be easing now. While Vestas’ reported average selling prices fluctuate each quarter due to variations in order mix — particularly between onshore and offshore projects, as well as regional differences — pricing is shown an encouraging trend over the past three and a half years. This can be seen as a shift toward more sustainable pricing strategies within the industry.

Review of Service business completed in 2024

Vestas’ service segment, traditionally a stable profit driver, faced a major setback in Q2 2024 after a significant write-off led to a sharp decline in margins. The company employs percentage-of-completion (POC) accounting for its service business, recognizing revenue based on incurred costs rather than billed amounts. This approach is not without controversy as it can temporarily boost revenues when incurred costs exceed initial estimates. But if total planned costs rise—reducing profit margins—revenues need to be adjusted downward. This was the case in Q2 2024, when higher-than-expected costs triggered a negative revenue adjustment.

This was a major setback for investor sentiment and confidence into the management team, which had to be addressed. Consequently, management launched a full review of the business. By the fourth quarter of 2024, the measures taken contributed to a notable improvement in profitability, with the service business returning to an 18% EBIT margin.

During the earnings call, management expressed confidence in achieving its long term EBIT margin target in the mid-20% range once again. A target that should be achievable given that the business generated an EBIT margin as high as 27.6% in 2021.

Management remains bullish on onshore business in the US

One of the biggest concerns is that the Trump administration could disrupt ongoing onshore wind projects in the U.S. through new regulations, permitting delays, or permit withdrawals. However, when asked about the impact of the shifting political landscape on Vestas' U.S. onshore business, CEO Henrik Andersen remained optimistic about its outlook for the foreseeable future:

"We see the onshore and the offshore very differently. I think the offshore has come to a stop more or less with immediate effect. But when it comes to the onshore backlog, we are well covered for '25. We are well covered a long distance into '26. And right now, I will say the -- the dialogue with customers is really, really well. So I'll be surprised if we don't continue. And also not to forget, the states generally appreciate the build-out and also need the electricity generation (...)

And I think right now affordability, the AI drive for demand in the US is so strong that there is more electricity and energy needed. And funny enough, the renewable sources are some of the fastest to build, and they are all well-positioned in the queue. And they are also, by the way, approved for all of the legislative credits (...)

On the onshore, very different. It's been part of legislation since 1992 and therefore the build-out is in many ways already committed and in the pipeline and at least a lot of the grids and the off-takers are relying on that offtake. So I think we talk about large balances of supply and demand and if you bring that into question, you actually run the risk of creating a small energy crisis if you don't get the build-out you expect "

Management's confidence was reinforced by CEO Henrik Andersen's purchase of Vestas shares following the election-driven sell-off, along with the company's decision to reinstate its dividend in 2025 and initiate a share buyback program.

Stock trading at low end of historical valuation

A decade-long analysis of VWSYF’s share price and its enterprise value to earnings before interest and taxes (EV/EBIT) multiple for the next fiscal year (FY2) demonstrates that Vestas is currently trading at the low end of its historical valuation. Excluding COVID-related distortions, the company’s shares traded at an average multiple of 10.8x from February 2015 to February 2020. Over the past 12 months, this multiple averaged 13x but has now declined to just 7.7x.

For 2025 the market's consensus estimate for VWSYF’s EBIT margin stands at 6.5% currently, positioning it at the upper end of the company’s official guidance range of 4-7%. Looking ahead, projections for 2026 and 2027 suggest an increase to 7.8% and 8.5%, respectively—figures that appear conservative given Vestas’ mid-term ambition to exceed a 10% margin. The company’s latest quarterly results bolster this case, having reported a margin of 12.9%.

Similarly, revenue growth estimates for 2026 and 2027 remain modest, at under 10% and around 6%, respectively. This is despite a record-high order backlog and the increasing urgency for renewable energy expansion, particularly within the European Union, to meet climate targets. While the 12.9% EBIT margin in the most recent quarter benefitted from many things going right as indicated by the management team, the figure demonstrates the feasibility of achieving its long term, double-digit margin target.

Using the street's probably conservative 2027 sales estimates and an EBIT margin ranging from 8.5% to 11% yield expected 2027 EBIT outcomes between EUR 1.92 and EUR 2.37 billion. To derive our share price target, we apply the historical 10.8x EV/EBIT multiple (FY2) and discount the result by 10%.

If VWSYF achieves a 9.5% EBIT margin in 2027, the implied share price target of 155 DKK will represent a potential 59% increase from the current stock price of DKK 98 (ca. USD 13.7).

Risk Considerations

Vestas faces several challenges that could significantly impact its future business trajectory and stock performance. While CEO Henrik Andersen considers the company to be well positioned for any tariff changes, a potential rise in tariffs could escalate input costs and adversely affect profitability.

The political climate in key markets also poses risks. The re-election of Donald Trump has introduced uncertainty, as his administration has expressed opposition to on- and offshore wind projects and has halted new federal permits in the U.S. President Donald Trump has consistently expressed his intention to dismantle the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), a significant climate and energy policy enacted during the Biden administration. However, the President cannot unilaterally repeal a law; such authority resides with Congress. Given the slim Republican majority in Congress and the fact that many Republican-led districts benefit from the IRA's provisions, achieving a complete repeal may be challenging. Besides, existing onshore wind projects are expected to be unaffected by President Trump's executive order.

In Germany, ongoing discussions about tightening permitting procedures and potentially reducing subsidies for onshore wind projects add another layer of complexity. Such regulatory changes could slow project approvals and diminish financial incentives, impacting Vestas's operations in the country.

The competitive landscape is intensifying, with Chinese wind turbine manufacturers expanding their global presence. These companies offer turbines at prices approximately 20% lower than their U.S. and European counterparts, potentially pressuring Vestas's market share and pricing strategies. Though, further Chinese inroads into Western markets might be hampered by the fact that energy assets are considered critical infrastructure.

Operational challenges are also evident. The 2025 EBIT guide disappointed markets, as the ramp up of the offshore business will impair Vestas profitability in 2025. If the ramp-up does not proceed as planned, potentially leading to higher costs and delayed revenue, it will weigh on profitability in the years to come. Additionally, the inherent seasonality of Vestas's operations, with a strong end to 2024, could result in a weak first quarter in 2025, potentially causing market apprehension upon the release of Q1 results.

Furthermore, while a review of the service business has been completed and management aims to restore profitability to a 25% target over the long term, execution risks remain. Inflation and increased repair costs have led to a one-time charge of €300 million in the service segment in 2024, underscoring the potential for further cost escalations and write-offs.

Final Thoughts

After years of strong investor enthusiasm driven by the market’s focus on ESG, Vestas may have previously been given too much credit for its growth ambitions and margin targets. However, sentiment now appears to have swung in the opposite direction. The company’s shares are currently trading at the lower end of their historical valuation range, and market consensus does not anticipate stronger top line growth nor a return to double-digit EBIT margins in the near future—creating the potential for an upside surprise.

The biggest risk remains political, particularly if the new U.S. administration introduces regulatory hurdles that slow the expansion of onshore wind energy. Ironically, an administration focused on reducing regulation could end up making it more difficult for businesses to develop new energy projects on private land. While such obstacles cannot be ruled out, the market may have become overly pessimistic about the wind energy sector’s prospects— which is why we think now is the right time to invest in the stock. BUY.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Innoviva: Underappreciated Strength In Hospital Therapeutics
data.description:
While most investors pay attention to biotech and pharma stocks with big pipeline hopes, Innoviva (NASDAQ:INVA) keeps its sights pinned on a solid portfolio of revenue-generating assets. Most known for the royalty stream from GSK (GSK) respiratory drugs, the company has aggressively entered infectious disease therapeutics as a player in a space often overlooked by the market. Given its strong balance sheet, defensive revenue base, and underappreciated catalysts, Innoviva presents an asymmetric risk-reward profile that is worth closer scrutiny.

Financial Situation

The latest financials from Innoviva paint an image of a transitioning company. The third quarter of 2024 generated $60.5 million in royalty revenue from GlaxoSmithKline compared to the prior year. While these legacy royalties remain the foundation of its business, the company is now focusing more on direct product sales. Net product sales increased to $19.7 million, with gains in GIAPREZA and XACDURO, with the latter up by 68% year-on-year. This shift signals that Innoviva is moving from a pure-play royalty collector to an operator in the specialty therapeutics space, with increased potential upside and increased execution risk.

The company has mixed net income despite these promising revenue trends. A drop from $82 million to $1.2 million in Q3 2024 and R&D investments weighing on margins suggest pressure on profitability. A $35.2 million loss on equity investments was mainly due to a declining stake in Armata Pharmaceuticals (ARMP). Although this has a non-operational impact, it highlights Innoviva's exposure to volatility in small-cap biotech holdings, an often-ignored risk in its valuation.

With $260.6 million in cash and equivalents, the company is well capitalized, but questions about cash burn arise from the sharp drop in net income. However, the push to commercialize Zevtera and to grow the company's specialty therapeutics division could push spending up. In my opinion, we should watch whether management tightens its financial discipline or leans further into growth mode - that will determine the company's trajectory over the next few years.

Key Assets in Innoviva’s Portfolio

Innoviva has an attractive asset portfolio that combines stable cash flows from its royalty business with a pipeline of emerging high-value specialty therapeutics. A royalty stream from GlaxoSmithKline's Breo Ellipta and Anoro Ellipta provides a robust revenue backbone and financial insulation not often seen in the biopharma space. This constant revenue enables the company to invest aggressively in its evolving drug portfolio, without dilutive equity raises or excessive debt. Royalties alone aren't sufficient to drive long-term value, so the company's strategic pivot to infectious disease therapeutics is critical.

Innoviva's specialty therapeutics division quietly built a set of assets in high-need areas. The vasoconstrictor GIAPREZA, indicated for distributive shock, has found a niche in critical care settings, and XACDURO, indicated for multidrug-resistant Acinetobacter infections, is gaining ground at a time of rising antimicrobial resistance concerns. Especially notable is the company's licensing deal for Zevtera, an advanced-generation cephalosporin antibiotic. With FDA approval for multiple indications including MRSA bacteremia, Zevtera may become a flagship product if Innoviva executes its commercialization plan.

Beyond its marketed products, the pipeline has been largely overlooked. New oral antibiotic zoliflodacin developed in collaboration with the Global Antibiotic Research & Development partnership may be the next big breakthrough in the treatment of gonorrhea, a disease becoming more resistant to current therapies. Although price pressures often keep antibiotics away from biotech-style valuations, new treatments in the landscape are being encouraged to target resistant pathogens. If Innoviva can take advantage of such dynamics, its emerging infectious disease portfolio could boost its valuation and act as a catalyst for growth beyond the slow but steady royalty business.

Zevtera: A Strategic Bet on Infectious Disease

With the licensing agreement for Zevtera, Innoviva moves from a royalty collector to an active player in specialty therapeutics. It gives Innoviva exclusive U.S. commercial rights to the advanced-generation cephalosporin antibiotic, recently approved by the FDA for high-need indications including Staphylococcus aureus bloodstream infections, right-sided infective endocarditis, acute bacterial skin and skin structure infections, and community-acquired bacterial pneumonia. Since more MRSA infections are resistant to traditional therapies now, Zevtera comes at a time when hospitals are pushing for more effective therapies, making it a potential market disruptor.

That move is part of a calculated shift in Innoviva's strategy. Instead of counting on organic growth in its existing portfolio, the company is leveraging its financial strength to buy assets with immediate commercial potential. The $4 million upfront payment to Basilea Pharmaceutica (OTC:BPMUF) is relatively modest for a drug with multiple approved indications and a clear path to market. Its structure, which includes tiered royalties and sales-based milestones, also indicates management believes it can accelerate adoption without going overboard financially. Like many pharma companies that find it expensive and risky to develop drugs, Innoviva is focusing on buying de-risked assets - a strategy in keeping with its focus on capital efficiency.

The problem is execution. A hospital-focused antibiotic requires significant investment in market access, provider education, and distribution - areas in which Innoviva has limited experience - in the United States. Though its success with XACDURO suggests a maturing competency in infectious disease commercialization, Zevtera competes with established alternatives. That said, I think if it can overcome those hurdles, Innoviva could potentially grab a significant share of the hospital antibiotic market. The real test will come mid-2025 when the company plans to market the drug - which could further solidify its evolving business model.

Balancing Opportunity with Execution Risk

Recent financial performance shows mounting commercialization costs as net income dropped year-over-year despite healthy revenue growth. Bringing drugs like Zevtera to market requires capital as well as experience in hospital sales, payer negotiations and formulary access - areas where Innoviva has a limited track record. The antibiotics market in particular is notoriously hard because of pricing constraints and long adoption cycles - which leaves Zevtera unclear as to whether it will deliver the kind of returns investors would expect from an FDA-approved product.

Uncertainty about Innoviva's investment strategy is another concern. Questions about the company's risky approach to equity holdings include its $35.2 million loss on equity holdings, driven largely by the falling value of its Armata Pharmaceuticals stake. Strategic investments in emerging biotech companies offer long-term upside, but also expose Innoviva to volatile swings in valuation that could compromise financial stability. Continuing to invest in outside biotech bets and increasing spending on commercial operations means cash reserves could erode faster than expected, and the company could face tough decisions about future investments.

But there are arguments against such risks. Innoviva has been able to grow without the diluted capital raises that plague many small biotech and pharma firms. The royalty stream from GSK remains an important safety net that can help cushion some of the unpredictability of drug commercialization. Additionally, infectious diseases are undergoing a reorientation with regulators providing new incentives for antibiotic development and commercialization. If Innoviva can weather these headwinds, it could profit from an underserved market and transform its business model without the existential risks of biotech pivots. However, the real question is whether management can scale its commercial infrastructure without losing the financial discipline that has defined the company historically.

Valuation

Innoviva has a mixed valuation outlook, but some metrics indicate it is undervalued relative to its long-term potential. One of the most compelling figures is the company's TTM EV/EBITDA of 6.28, well below the sector median of 14.87. That suggests the market is either undervaluing its current revenue streams or discounting its future in specialty therapeutics. With its stable royalty income and growing product sales, the discount seems excessive given the company's continuing commercial expansion. With a successful execution of management's infectious disease strategy, several expansions are possible, bringing Innoviva's valuation closer to sector peers.

The TTM Price-to-Cash Flow ratio of 6.94, 58.61% lower than the sector median of 16.76, suggests that despite ongoing investments in commercialization, Innoviva generates cash flow at a rate that makes its current valuation look attractive. A healthy cash flow position gives the company flexibility to fund growth without relying on dilutive capital raises. Having $260.6 million in cash and equivalents, Innoviva has a buffer that few small biotech and pharma firms have and that supports its case that the market is discounting its ability to manage growth while maintaining financial stability. Those concerns about execution remain valid, but the current valuation offers upside if the company shows progress in monetizing its burgeoning portfolio.

Future Outlook

Innoviva is unusual in the biopharma space - financially sound, cash-generating yet fundamentally changing its business model. A royalty stream from GlaxoSmithKline provides a solid foundation on which the company can build a calculated expansion into specialty therapeutics without the high-risk, high-dilution pathway common in the industry. Its advancing portfolio of critical care and infectious disease drugs, including Zevtera and XACDURO, places it in a segment seeing increasing demand driven by antimicrobial resistance concerns. The real challenge lies in execution: commercializing hospital-focused drugs requires infrastructure and expertise that Innoviva is still developing. But the company's ability to buy de-risked assets rather than speculative R&D compounds mitigates some of that uncertainty.

Its favorable valuation metrics show that the market is discounting its growth potential compared to peers. Though worries over falling net income and investment losses sap sentiment, the company has ample cash on its hands. Innoviva may see a meaningful re-rating if management is able to grow its infectious disease portfolio without losing financial discipline.

data.title: Ryanair: Keeping Costs Steady While Investing In Growth
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Arav Kacker as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction and Ryanair’s Value Proposition

At its core, the Ryanair (NASDAQ:RYAAY) (OTCPK:RYAOF) story can be summarized in a single phrase: the ability to run a tight ship. In a year so rife with struggling low-cost airlines. Notably, Southwest and Spirit’s respective issues, it would not be unreasonable to call into question the long-term feasibility of low-cost and ultra-low-cost carriers (‘LCCs’ and ‘ULCCs’). With that in mind, though, Ryanair persists as the gold standard in the industry, consistently performing head and shoulders above the pack and showing no signs of relinquishing its lead. Through the analysis of Ryanair’s operations and financials, both as a standalone company and in relation to the rest of the market, the combination of a convincing growth story paired with robust numbers to back it up will elucidate the long-term opportunity that Ryanair presents.

The Growth Story

The LCC and ULCC business model is about little more than sheer volume, and Ryanair is no exception. The average stage length of a Ryanair flight in FY24 was 780 miles (for reference, the distance from Dublin to Berlin is roughly 827 miles) - an entirely different game than, for example, transatlantic carriers (London-New York comes in around 3,500 miles).

Mechanically, it is first necessary to delve into the computation of Available Seat Miles [ASM] to appreciate growth prospects. Based on the way that Ryanair reports operational metrics, ASM is: total sectors flown * avg. aircraft capacity * avg. sector length. For ease of analysis, the most intuitive way to consider ASM here would be (avg. sector length * avg. aircraft capacity) * total sectors flown. In essence, avg. sector length and avg. aircraft capacity can be thought of as more or less constant (with the caveat that avg. sector length has crept up from 761 miles in FY20 to 780 miles in FY24), as short routes and small narrow body aircraft (mainly B737 variants, in the case of Ryanair) are integral to LCC/ULCC operations. Growth, therefore, comes overwhelmingly from total sectors flown - a product of the fleet size and number of routes flown.

Key Growth Drivers

Firstly, fleet size. Ryanair’s investment in 300 new 737 Max 10s is considerable, increasing fleet size by almost 50%. On top of this, the Max 10s also offer a performance boost, cutting fuel consumption by 16%. The Max 10s also boast a +21% increase on Ryanair’s current capacity (220 seats vs Ryanair’s current average of 190, which is largely driven by 411x B737-Next Gens at 189 seats, pulled up by 172x ‘Gamechangers’ at 192 seats).

Next, airports. Ryanair currently serves 235 airports (as of FY24, up from 222 in FY23 but down from 242 in FY20), or just under 80% of the approx. 294 total European airports currently operating B737s. Granted, Ryanair makes the intentional decision to not fly out of certain airports (instead preferring secondary locations), and some may simply not present viable routes, but it seems reasonable to assume at least some growth potential through new routes. To this end, Ryanair CEO O’Leary has already expressed his desire to be the first airline to resume operations to Ukraine following the resolution of the conflict.

Finally, routes. Although the number of routes operated by Ryanair is not readily available, this site, citing OAG, revealed that Ryanair operated “over 1,750 routes” in the last week of November 2022. Taking that at face value (and, admittedly, disregarding the impact of seasonality on routes, for lack of alternative), it becomes possible to somewhat contextualize new route growth. Per this source, Ryanair added 553 routes in 2021, 302 in 2022, and 222 in 2023. In 2024, multiple sources assert that Ryanair added 208 routes in 2024, so the ~200/year figure seems a reasonable assumption (net of COVID-19-related re-openings and a handful of closures each year). That’s a healthy 11% over the 2022 figure, and no doubt a significant contributor to ASM growth, something which will likely benefit further from the fleet investment.

Comparables, Peers, and the (lack of) Competition

Turning to other players in the LCC/ULCC market, there are a number of relevant airlines across the globe:

They can broadly be grouped by region: Southwest and Frontier in NA, Volaris in LATAM, AirAsia, Cebu, Spring Airlines, and InterGlobe Aviation (IndiGo) in Asia, and Ryanair, easyJet, Jet2, Wizz Air, Pegasus, and Norwegian Air Shuttle in Europe. Although a closer look at trading multiples and valuation more broadly is to come, it is first to consider operational metrics (as of FY23 for consistency, though this will be updated once FY24 results are complete across the board).

Firstly, Ryanair’s crown jewel - load factor. Where Ryanair comes in at 93.5% yield, the mean and median of the peers, respectively, is 86.4% and 86.9%. The closest competitor is Jet2 at 91%, but no other airline even broke 90%. Going back to the core idea of running a tight ship, Ryanair’s pricing and ticketing strategy is by far the most effective in the market. Speaking anecdotally, it would certainly be a challenge to find a single year when any airline beat Ryanair’s 2020 (March-31 FYE) load factor of 95.2%. In terms of revenue per ASM (currency adj.), Ryanair’s 5.37c sits above the average of 5.06 and median of 3.75, but this is somewhat misleading as some airlines simply report total revenues rather than a split of passenger fees and ancillary revenues. As such, looking at TRASM makes more sense, and Ryanair’s 8.35c comes in a tick above the average of 8.29c, and just below the median of 8.68c.

Turning to costs per ASM, Ryanair again emerges superior, demonstrating more effective cost management than peers. For total CASM, Ryanair’s 7.23c is over half a dollar cheaper than the average of 7.89c and a dollar cheaper than the 8.24c median. Examining costs in closer detail, CASM ex-fuel is where Ryanair really shines, coming at 4.11c vs the average of 5.16c and median of 4.92c. This is reflected in margins, where Ryanair’s 13.4% EBIT blows away the average of 6.3% and median of 7.8%. Although fuel costs are a systemic issue across the industry, Ryanair runs a markedly more streamlined operation when it comes to other operating expenses. To wrap up this section - although these figures paint a pretty picture when it comes to Ryanair, it would be remiss to not mention the inescapable impact of different regions and currencies when looking at this peer set.

Intrinsic Valuation

For this DCF, I prioritized simplicity and sought to minimize assumptions and unnecessary frills. The purpose of this exercise, in my opinion, was to guide directionally and sanity-check the growth drivers discussed earlier - not find some magic number. As illustrated below, my ‘bull case’ model implied an 86% premium over the current price, but I will tinker with some of the key assumptions to elucidate a broader range of values.

Key Inputs and Assumptions

First and foremost - revenues. TRASM is held constant, revenue set to grow in line with Ryanair’s guidance, using ASM as a proxy measure of passengers. The 3.7% CAGR is not unreasonable and very much in line with historicals as well as growth plans. Dropping that to 3%/year still yields a 47% premium, and 2%/year a 32% premium.

Next, D&A and Capex. Admittedly, I largely opted for the 'quick and dirty' approach to forecasting here, simply looking at historical depreciation and capex to guide projections. I tied D&A expense to a % of capex (the <100% at the end of the forecast period is justifiable on account of growth requirement), and will briefly explore the effect of varying capex requirements and D&A spend on implied value.

There is a lot of flexibility built-in with respect to Capex spend. For example, if the step was tripled to +300mm/year, the implied premium still comes in almost 38%. Similarly, the D&A as a % of Capex leaves plenty of margin - with a 10%-90% range yielding a premium of 76%-92%.

Lastly, fuel expense. Intuitively, I have tied this to ASM, like most of the other income statement line items:

This is likely the most vulnerable assumption in the model. In the interest of practicality and academic integrity, I have used a trailing 3-year average moving forward, implicitly placing some degree of faith in Ryanair's fuel hedging team. Although this has been substantiated somewhat by FY26 and FY27 hedging activities already (covered in Section 6), I am most certainly not a commodities expert. If I use a L5Y average (2.5%) the implied value shoots up to 118%, and if I use the last year only (3.4%), it plummets to just 6.8%. There can be no argument or rationalization that minimizes this risk.

Relative Valuation

As mentioned earlier, this is not a perfect set of comps. That said, it serves to highlight Ryanair's relative pricing. Although it trades at a slight premium on an EV/Sales basis, I see value in the EV/EBITDA and EV/EBIT (more so the latter, given the significance of D&A with respect to airlines) multiples as they are reflective of Ryanair's solid operating margin. I will note the huge premium over comps on a UFCF (FCFF) basis, but will caveat that this is skewed by a number of comparables not even being FCFF-positive.

Ryanair trades at 45% of the average and just 72% of the median on an EV/EBIT basis, implying considerable upside.

The Elephant in the Room … Fuel (and other risks to consider)

There’s a saying that essentially posits that ‘investing in airlines is just investing in oil’, and it’s most certainly a valid concern. Within the set of comparables previously identified, the average fuel expense as a % of revenues comes in at 32.5% (FY23), and Ryanair sits at 37.4%. There is a relatively wide spread between the other European players (easyJet @ 24.9%, Jet2 @ 10.4%, Wizz Air @ 50.2%, Norwegian Air Shuttle @ 30.2%), but it is ultimately clear that Ryanair is on the higher end.

For the most part, it appears that Ryanair does a relatively consistent job of hedging against significant fluctuations in oil price, but FY24 does raise an eyebrow. Also, 25E and 26E prices are 85% and 75% hedged already, respectively, at much more attractive prices (shown vs. FY24 price)

Generally, Ryanair’s ability to efficiently manage ex-fuel costs does afford them a little more flexibility with respect to fuel costs. Furthermore, the increased fuel efficiency of the B737 Max 10s that will be added to the fleet in the coming years will help combat operating costs. Regardless, it is by far the most pressing concern with respect to Ryanair’s operations, as well as the most difficult to forecast/speculate on. At the end of the day, the question at hand is whether the upside potential outweighs the inevitable risk associated with fuel.

The other risk that poses a material threat to Ryanair is supply delays. Boeing has faced a number of challenges recently - most notably with respect to their Max 9 program. Although there is no explicit reason to expect that the Max 10 program will fail to receive certification on the current timeline (~mid-2025), it goes without saying that any delays or challenges pertaining to the Max 10 program, or Boeing’s production line as a whole, would severely hamper Ryanair’s growth.

Conclusion and Closing Thoughts

At the end of the day, I like Ryanair's business model. Although I see value in the combination of top-line growth and prudent cost management, the Ryanair story, in my eyes, is not one that is overly reliant on massive revenue growth. Anything over a 2-3% steady growth will yield disproportionally positive results, assuming continued operational efficiency - which is why I am willing to accept the systemic risk of fuel prices and supplier issues. I am satisfied the flexibility built into my model allows enough margin of safety to reasonably take a bullish view on the company, and the trading multiples echo this sentiment.

Although I see this as a long-term value play, there are a few short-term catalysts I will be keeping an eye out for. Boeing is expected to receive FAA certification on the 737 Max 10 sometime this year, which would certainly help to alleviate one of the big risks facing this thesis. Similarly, Delta should be the first airline to receive a 737 Max 10 order (albeit only next year), but the reasoning is the same. When I think about price targets and fair value, I see that 6.8% premium / $48.90 case arising from prolonged high fuel costs as a bear/base case, and the initial 'base' model implying 86.1% upside / $85.12 as the bull case.

Finally, if you've made it this far - thank you for reading this! I welcome any and all feedback.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Week Ahead: Tariffs Loom And The State Of The Trump Trade
data.description:
Many observers seem confused. They express disappointment that the so-called "Trump trade", a rally in the dollar, has not materialized. Yet, in some ways, isn't that what happened from late last September, ironically around the Federal Reserve's 50 bp rate cut, around the time investors began taking US election polls more seriously? In Q4 24, the Federal Reserve's real broad trade weighted dollar index rose by 4.9%, the best quarterly performance since Q4 2016. From late last September through a week before President Trump's second inauguration, the Dollar Index rose by about 10%. Since January 10-13, the "Trump trade" in the foreign exchange has been unwinding. With momentum indicators stretched and the US tariff threat about to enter a new phase next month, a key question is how much more of the greenback's gains will be unwound? We have suggested that it is almost over and that still seems to be a fair assessment.

The data highlights in the week ahead are unlikely to alter expectations for central bank policy and may pale in significance compared to the US tariffs that may be implemented as soon as next month. It is now well appreciated that the stronger than expected rise in the US CPI and PPI will not be echoed in the measure of prices that the Fed explicitly targets, headline PCE deflator. Tokyo's February CPI, which is a good gauge of changes in the national figure that is reported with a several week lag, is expected to moderate as energy subsidies were renewed. Excluding fresh food and energy, Tokyo's CPI may have edged up to 2.0% from 1.9%. Canada reports December and Q4 24 GDP. The economy contracted by 0.2% in November (+0.3% in October). However, US tariffs as early as March 4 will likely be more important for the interest rate and policy outlook. China reports its February PMI on March 1. A small increase is expected.

US

Drivers: Between the assorted tariff threats and peace talks to end Russia's war in Ukraine, without involving Kyiv or Brussels, and assorted acts from petty, like renaming a body of water, to the reckless, like unilateral disarmament by ending USAID and other soft-power projections, the US has become the main disruptive force in the world. Although many have suggested “tariff fatigue," the US tariff threat enters a new phase in March. The 25% tariffs on Canada and Mexico, ostensibly over immigration and fentanyl will be revisited in early March. Steel and aluminum tariffs are threatened for March 12. The reciprocal tariffs and auto tariffs may be a few weeks later. Investors, businesses, and economists continue to wrestle with potential supply shock on one hand, with inflationary implications, and the consequential weaker demand on the other hand. Moreover, the focus on the tariff threats is sucking the oxygen from other issues. In particular, the move on immigration and firing of thousands of federal government workers will likely have profound implications for the labor market, consumption, and growth.

Data: The higher-than-expected CPI and PPI readings were noisy. The real implication is that the headline and core PCE deflators likely slowed. The headline January PCE deflators may come in at 2.5%, down from 2.6%, while the core looks like 2.6% compared with 2.8% in December. Meanwhile, personal income and consumption looks to have slowed sequentially in January to a still respectable 0.3% month-over-month pace. Alongside the January durable goods orders will help give a better sense of the pace of growth in Q1 25. January housing starts will feed into residential investment. Halfway through the quarter, and the Atlanta Fed's model has the economy tracking a 2.3% annualized pace, roughly the same as Q4 24. There are a bevy of regional Fed surveys, and the Conference Board's consumer consider survey (expect some deterioration), but they typically have negligible impact.

Prices: The Dollar Index met the (38.2%) retracement objective of the rally from the low last September (~100.15) near 106.35 last week. The next retracement (50%) is near 105.15. The five- and 20-day moving averages are falling, and while stretched, the momentum indicators have not turned up. Still, with a new phase in the US tariff threat beginning next month, we continue to look for a reversal pattern or some other price action to indicate that the downtrend since January 13, when the Dollar Index peaked (~100.15) is over. A move above 107.40 would improve the technical tone.

EMU

Drivers: The US tariff threats, its peace talks with Russia, and the appearance of officials (e.g., Vance and Musk) interfering in the German election is causing a crisis in the Atlantic alliance that has not been seen since at least the 1956 Suez Crisis. One of the implications is that between the costs rebuilding Ukraine and boosting defense spending, the supply of long-term European bonds will most likely have to increase. Russia's invasion of Ukraine was disruptive for the EU and the possibility of the end of the war appeared to help lift the euro initially. The swaps market has settled down and looks for two more ECB rate cuts in H1 25 and one in the second half. The results of the Germany election will be known before the markets open on Sunday. There is little doubt that the CDU will come out ahead but is unlikely to secure an outright majority. An alliance with the SPD and Greens looks likely. The AfD may come in second place but in the parliamentary system second place does not ensure it will be asked to join the government. And in this case, it will not.

Data: The economic calendar is light on market moving high-frequency data. Credit figures released with the January money supply may show continued improvement on the margin. The EC confidence surveys typically do not elicit much reaction. The ECB's one- and three-year inflation surveys are also of passing interest, which for the record stood at 2.8% and 2.4%, respectively at the end of last year.

Prices: The euro tested two-month highs last week and they held. The euro reached nearly $1.0525 on January 27 and $1.0515 on February 14 (following the unexpectedly poor US retail sales and decline in manufacturing output). It peaked near $1.0505 last week. Support was tested around $1.04 in the middle of last week. Only a break of that range is important. The US tariff threat enters a new phase next month and there is little doubt in the market's mind that the ECB will cut rates again on March 6.

China

Drivers: Contrary to speculation, Beijing has not attempted to offset the recent new 10% US tariff on Chinese imports with currency depreciation. The dollar peaked against the yuan a week and a half before Trump's inauguration. China wants neither a strong nor weak yuan, but one that is broadly stable against the US dollar. Many observers see China as a primary beneficiary of recent US actions, including the freeze of foreign aid, the closing of USAID, and the broad tariff threat. For its part, China has announced it was eliminating tariffs on the imports from the world's poorest countries. At the same time, Xi appears to be following Napoleon's advice: "Never interrupt your enemy when he is making a mistake." This is not a partisan assessment but from Beijing's perspective, the US is wreaking havoc and discord through its alliance and unilaterally disarming some of the elements of its power projection.

Data: China's domestic economy still needs support, but the PBOC does not appear to be poised to cut rates or reserve requirements now. The one-year Medium-Term Lending Facility rate has been downgraded as a key benchmark, but the lending volume may increase. On March 1, China reports the February PMI. Recall that in January, the manufacturing PMI fell to 49.1 (from 50.1), a five-month low and matching the 2024 low seen in February and again in August. The non-manufacturing PMI fell to 50.2 (from 52.2). It has not been below 50 since December 2022. The composite stood at 50.1 in January, matching the 2024 low set in August.

Prices: The dollar made a marginal two-month low against near CNH7.23 last week and rebounded ahead of the weekend to settle at CNH7.2550. Still, the dollar slipped for the third consecutive week, matching the longest decline in four years. Contrary to expectations, the yuan has strengthened by about 1% since the US announced its new 10% tariff on imports from China. The US 10-year premium over China is less than 275 bp, the least in two months, and the index of mainland companies that trade in Hong Kong are up nearly 19% this year. Meanwhile, the PBOC continues to set the dollar's reference rate in a narrow range ensuring the yuan remains relatively stable against it.

Japan

Drivers: The exchange rate seems to be buffeted by two main forces: the US interest rates and the BOJ monetary policy. Its appearance as a safe haven seems to be more a function of the use of the yen as a funding currency (carry trades) and through the US interest rate channel. Last week, developments in Japan seemed to be the more crucial factor. Provided the economy and prices evolve as the BOJ expects, Governor Ueda is committed to normalizing Japanese monetary policy, and indeed that is what is happening. The dollar peaked against the yen last month the same day it peaked against the Chinese yuan (January 10) but the rolling 60-day correlation between the two has weakened sharply since the carry trade unwind last July, from around 0.80 to now near 0.20. It is not clear the Trump administration will grant Japan exemptions from its metals and reciprocal tariff threats. Japan records an overall trade deficit but a bilateral surplus with the US.

Data: Japan reports January industrial production and retail sales both are likely to improve sequentially (from 0.3% and -0.7%, respectively), but the most important data point is Tokyo's February CPI. It signals the general development of the national CPI figure, which will not be reported for several weeks. The headline rate stood at 3.4% in January, the highest since April 2023, and was flattered by the end of household energy subsidies before the new subsidies kicked in. Fresh food prices include rice in Japan and rice prices have been steadily increasing, and this poses upside risks for Tokyo CPI. Excluding fresh food and energy, Tokyo's inflation has been hovering between 1.8%-1.9% for four months through January.

Prices: The dollar met the (50%) retracement objective of the rally from the mid-September 2024 low (~JPY39.60) found near JPY149.20 and slipped below JPY149 ahead of the weekend. That was greenback's low since early last December when it bottomed around JPY148.65. Although the exchange rate is often more correlated with changes in the US 10-year Treasury yield, the first half of last week seemed more about JGB yields and the spread, which narrowed to almost 300 bp, the least in three months. In the middle of last September, when the dollar was forging an important low, the spread briefly narrowed inside 280 bp. The US 10-year yield slumped 17 bp in the past three sessions to 4.40%. The dollar has fallen by about 6.25% against the yen since peaking on January 10. The momentum indicators are stretched but have not turned higher. A move above JPY151 might help stabilize the technical tone, but the dollar must regain the JPY152.40-60 area to lift the outlook.

UK

Drivers: Sterling tumbled nearly 10% from late September through mid-January. The fall and subsequent recovery tracked the US dollar movements broadly. Sterling's rolling 60-day inverse correlation with changes in the Dollar Index is near a three-year-high, a little below 0.70. The rolling 60-day correlation of changes in sterling and euro reached a six-month high near 0.85 last month and is now slightly below there. The UK two-year yield is back at the same level as the US two-year yield. It was a six-basis point premium a week ago. The UK's 10-year premium rose to 11 bp, the upper end of where it has traded in nearly four months.

Data: The UK data is of tertiary interest. The February CBI reported sales and Nationwide house price index do not capture much attention from market participants. The swaps market sees little chance of another BOE rate cut next month, but strongly (~82%) favors one in May. Prime Minister Starmer's visit to Washington may swamp the data. However, the British-French proposal for 30k-European "reassurance force" will look too much like a NATO operation for Moscow to find it acceptable. And the British Prime Minister may find it even more difficult to alter America's new strategic path, which includes tariffs. Fortunately, US and UK bilateral trade is sufficiently confusing that both sides can show a small bilateral surplus.

Prices: Sterling rose to a new two-month high before the weekend, near $1.2680. It was the third consecutive weekly advance, which matches its longest advance since last April. It met the (38.2%) retracement of the nearly 10% Q4 24 slide that carried into mid-January, which was found around $1.2610. The next retracement (50%) is near $1.2765 and the 200-day day moving average is slightly below $1.2790. Sterling has been bumping against its upper Bollinger Band (~$1.2680 before the weekend), and while the momentum indicators are over-extended, they have not turned down. Still, the close was poor, near session lows set late in the session. A break of $1.2550 weakens the technical tone.

Canada

Drivers: The US dollar was driven to nearly CAD1.48 when it seemed like a 25% tariff was about to be slapped on all of Canada's exports to the US but energy, which would face a 10% levy. Such a shock to the Canadian economy was expected to spur the Bank of Canada to cut rates more aggressively. When the postponement was announced, Canada's two-year discount to the US narrowed and the Canadian dollar strengthened. The markets wobbled on the US threat of 25% duties on aluminum and steel, for which Canada is the largest foreign supplier, but the implementation date--March 12--was seen to allow time for negotiations and this saw a rather muted market reaction. Ideas that the US CPI and PPI overstated the price pressures measured by the PCE deflator, which the Fed targets, and the disappointing January retail sales and manufacturing output spurred a broader US dollar pullback, and the Canadian dollar rose to new highs since mid-December.

Data: Canada reports December and Q4 24 GDP figures. The data is too old, given the recent developments to have much impact. That said, growth likely accelerated to around 1.5%-1.6% at an annualized rate from 1.0% in Q3 24. However, the composition looks different. Consumption and government spending likely slowed, but fixed investment and net exports appear to have improved. Since Q4 22, Canada has recorded a small (less than 1% of GDP) current account deficit, and even that has been trending lower since Q3 23. However, the Q4 24 shortfall may have more than doubled from 0.33% of GDP in Q3 24. The swaps, market is discounting almost of 50% chance of a cut at the next Bank of Canada meeting on March 12.

Prices: The greenback appears to have forged a near-term shelf above CAD1.4150. The upside has been settled firmly even if a little below last week's high (~CAD1.4245. The momentum indicators look poised to turn higher. Above the CAD1.4250-60 area, the next hurdle is around CAD1.4300. Overcoming that cap could spur a move back into the CAD1.44-CAD1.45 area.

Australia

Drivers: The rolling 60-day inverse correlation of changes in the Australian dollar and Dollar Index has eased from around 0.70 around the US election in early November to slightly less than 0.55 at the end of last week. It has firmed slightly over the past couple of weeks. On the other hand, the 60-day correlation of changes in the Australian dollar and Canadian dollar is slightly above 0.70. Australia's two-year yield discount to the US exceeded 50 bp earlier this month, the most since last July. It is now less than 35 bp. It was briefly at a premium in late September 2024, around when the Aussie peaked near $0.6940. Prime Minister Albanese hoped that Australia would be granted an exemption from US steel and aluminum tariffs, but Trump trade advisor Navarro argued forcefully against it.

Data: Australia's January monthly CPI print is due. The central bank still puts more stock in the quarterly reading. Australia's monthly CPI bottomed last September and October at 2.1%. It rose in November and December to finish the year at 2.5%. In January 2024, the CPI stood at 3.4%. While the RBA began its easing cycle last week, it sounded cautious. The market does not have the next cut fully discounted until July.

Prices: The push to almost $0.6410 before the weekend, the Australian dollar's high since mid-December nearly met the (38.2%) retracement of the slide from (~$0.6940) last September to about ($0.6090) in early February. The (50%) retracement is another cent higher. The Aussie flirts with the upper Bollinger Band (~$0.6400), and while the momentum indicators are stretched, they have not turned down. It settled near session lows recorded in late North American turnover near $0.6350. Support is seen near $.6330.

Mexico

Drivers: The US tariff threats roiled the peso initially, but subsequent threats, like on steel and aluminum, had noticeably less impact. Still, postponement of the 25% tariff due to undocumented immigration and fentanyl may still come into force in early March. Steel and aluminum tariffs could be implemented on March 12, and the auto tariffs are threatened for early April. In addition, the US has threatened to designate Mexico's drug cartels as "terrorist organizations," which would empower the US military. Mexican President Sheinbaum has threatened to retaliate by expanding a lawsuit against US gun manufacturers.

Data: Mexico's economic calendar has three highlights in the last week of the month. First is the inflation report for the first half of February. Mexico's inflation is back within the target range, the economy has slowed, and US tariff threats are potentially a powerful headwind. Second, Mexico reports January trade figures. Mexico recorded a trade surplus last year of about $170 bln with the US. Yet, an estimated 30% to 40% of Mexico's exports to the US originate from affiliates of US companies. At the lower end of estimates, Mexico's trade surplus all but disappears. That underscores our sense that US corporate foreign direct investment strategy is a key target of the new US administration's tariff policy. Third, Mexico reports January unemployment figures. Unemployment has risen in Januarys for the last 19 years without exception.

Prices: Like the Canadian dollar, the peso has strengthened for three consecutive weeks. They both recovered in full from the tariff-threat sell-off earlier this month. Both seem vulnerable to the new tariff phase next month. Last week's low was near MXN20.20 and the January low was closer to MXN20.1350. The momentum indicators are stretched but also have not turned higher. A move above MXN20.48-MXN20.50 would lift the tone.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: Worst Day Of 2025
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 notched two new record highs but also logged its worst day of the year — all in the same week. The index is now 2.13% below its record close from February 19th, 2025, and is up 2.46% year-to-date.

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index over the past 5 days:

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

As of February 21st, the U.S. Treasury put the closing yield on the 10-year note at 4.42% and the 2-year note at 4.19%. See our latest Treasury Snapshot here. ETFs associated with Treasuries include: iShares 1-3 Year Treasury Bond ETF (SHY), iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF), and iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT).

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached a then all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53. This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new then all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent selloffs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

Here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been above its 200-day moving average since November 1st, 2023, and above the 50-day moving average since February 4th.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range. I've also included a 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. On December 18th, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (3.45%) since November 30, 2022 (3.59%).

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is a market cap-weighted index which includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently up 2.46% year-to-date, while the S&P Equal Weight is up 2.73% year-to-date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).
