data.title: Udemy On The Rise: Inside Its Game-Changing Strategy To Dominate EdTech
data.description:
Udemy, Inc. (NASDAQ:UDMY) is a $1.5 billion edtech company that operates an online marketplace platform connecting consumers with tens of thousands of experts in different subject matters for the purposes of skill acquisition, development, as well as validation.

Last week, Udemy reported strong results that indicate a boost in its enterprise-facing business, which have contributed to expanded profit margins, and increased growth opportunities.

In the wake of the AI proliferation boom, and the dynamic shifts impacting conventional educational landscapes, the entire space Udemy functions within is being drastically redefined. As a result, Udemy is shifting gears, and changing its strategy to increase its focus on the enterprise side of its business, whilst simultaneously refining its products across the platform through AI-powered tools.

With a massive growth potential, and strong fundamentals, I believe Udemy is a steal at its current price.

I rate the stock as a strong buy.

A Look at Recent Performance

On Friday last week, Udemy released its Q4 and full 2024 results, and the market was clearly impressed, considering that the stock underwent a single-day jump of over 28%. While revenue in 2024 jumped by about 8%, the enterprise side of the business actually climbed by an impressive 18%. Adjusted EBITDA jumped from $7.8 million in 2023 to a staggering $43 million in 2024. The company further expects this figure to jump to as high as $85 million by the end of 2025. For context, by as recently as in 2022, Udemy reported an adjusted EBITDA loss of almost $50 million. This achievement really is significant, and demonstrates excellent execution on the part of the company's management (which owns over 47% of the company!)

Now that I have given a broad rundown on the most significant aspects of the company's performance, let me individually go over some of the dimensions and opportunities surrounding Udemy, that I find most compelling.

There's a Massive Opportunity in Enterprise

In its recent earnings call, Udemy's leadership has been pretty explicit about how it believes its enterprise customers, particularly large enterprises, represent "the greatest opportunity for growth, and the highest returns in investment". This is, of course, due to the improved business economics this segment of the market provides. Not only do they have higher cross-sell and upsell potential, but also boast substantially higher rates of retention.

Annualized Recurring Revenue for enterprise was at $517 million for 2024, compared to $466 million in 2023. Moreover, net dollar retention rate was 98%, while for larger customers the rate was at 103%, as a result of cross-sell. Also, Udemy Business customers increased to almost 17,100 during this period, while adjusted gross margin expanded to 75%:

The company's enterprise customer base includes 51% of Fortune 100 companies, and 72% of the Nikkei 225. In Q4, Udemy reported some pretty notable logo wins, which include Nasdaq, Dropbox, Kellanova, among others.

I believe, moving forward, enterprise customers will continue driving growth for Udemy, especially as organizations across the globe brace for the AI shift. Given the pace of change AI has resulted in, in just a few years, I think it would not be farfetched to assume that enterprises across most sectors and industries will look very different. This creates a massive opportunity in terms of reskilling and upskilling workforces, and Udemy's enterprise facing business is positioned very favorably to capitalize on this.

Remarkably, this phenomenon extends beyond tech companies and IT firms. For instance, IKEA Retail last year announced its plans to deliver AI literacy training to over 30,000 employees, and 500 management personnel. There have also been reports about similar programs being rolled out by companies such as S&P Global, Mastercard, and JPMorgan Chase. Of course, these programs come as part of a broader trend where AI proficiency continues to become critical, and given the pace of change in tech-based systems, consistent training programs on tech updates may become much more of a norm, moving forward.

Udemy is already seeing a shift as a result of these changing dynamics that continue to be defined by rapid AI advancements. The company's management highlighted that 2024 saw an almost 10x increase in the number of generative AI courses for the year. Coupled with its increased focus on the enterprise side of its business, I think Udemy is orienting itself to capture a booming opportunity.

In fact, of its installed large customer base on the enterprise side, the company reports that it has achieved less than 10% of penetration. The company further estimates that, the opportunity at 50% penetration of the existing large customer base alone is worth $1.5 billion.

Moreover, it is also important to keep in mind that Udemy provides its services to over 5000 large enterprises. According to Capital IQ, there are globally over 130,000 enterprise companies that have over 1000 employees. The upside potential is beyond substantial in my opinion, and Udemy is positioned among the most clear beneficiaries here.

Finally, I would also like to touch upon an area that, I believe, most people tend to overlook. With AI fast dominating the mainstream, the need for soft skills, such as communication techniques, leadership, negotiation skills, creativity are as critical as ever. In fact, a 2023 study found that as AI adoption will dominate the technical side of things and automate many of the processes, the most sought-after employees will be those who excel in soft skills—such as creative problem-solving, ethical judgment, and effective communication. Ultimately, I believe that attaining this 'human edge' would become far more important for companies than ever before.

Strengthening its Moat Through AI

I believe that Udemy's moat ultimately lies in its instructor-driven marketplace structure. Unlike the overwhelming majority of its competitors, Udemy is not a content publisher, but is rather a service provider that operates a network platform of over 80,000 subject-matter experts. Each of these instructors are incentivized to continuously create and update high-quality learning content. This structure provides Udemy's platform with an unparalleled speed-to-market advantage that makes it superior to other learning platforms.

It is evident that, moving forward, Udemy plans on leveraging this advantage to further strengthen its moat. The company plans to refine its offerings through the use of AI-powered tools, interactive assessments such as role-plays, and other dynamic features. In fact, this transition is so crucial for the company that the founder, Eren Bali, has re-joined Udemy as Chief Technology Officer after a decade away from the wheel, in order to help in setting the direction in the new, AI-led age. This is highly significant, in my opinion, because it indicates that the company plans on doubling down on the idea that innovation remains at the heart of Udemy's growth story.

During the earnings call, Bali made multiple references to 'AI-powered role-specific learning paths', and it seems like the company is bent upon taking the learning experience to a whole new level, which ultimately will strengthen Udemy's moat.

Secondly, I think there's another, more deeper dimension to the future of platforms like Udemy, that a lot of us tend to overlook when pouring over immediate metrics. This, of course, is about how there is a real shift underway, in many places across the globe, towards a skills-based learning model, which could potentially challenge the institution of universities.

Now before you say to yourself, "here comes another one of those 'college degrees are useless' pundits", here me out. I won't be typing out an entire essay to make my point, but rather would just mention a few facts for you to consider:

  1. On average, a college degree costs around $38,000 a year. Most students seeking college degrees have to take loans in order to attain a university education, with the idea that the cost would be paid off over time with the higher employability the college claims to ensure.
  2. There's a growing trend among corporations of ditching college degree requirements for salaried jobs. According to CNBC, 1 in 3 US companies no longer require formal degrees for corporate jobs, and are instead prioritizing relevant skill sets. This chips away the need for students to take hundreds of thousands of dollars in debt, and pay them off for decades, when they could attain the skills for a few hundred dollars on a platform like Udemy.
  3. College degrees are no longer seen as the key to improving social mobility, as they were in the 60s and 70s. In 1960, only 7.7% of Americans had a college degree, whereas by 2021, this figure rose to 39%. We are clearly heading towards an oversaturation in the market, and most people attempt to combat this by increasing their number of degrees. Despite this, graduates of 'college alternative programs' report much more positive hiring figures than university students.

Given these shifting dimensions, I believe that Udemy is very well positioned to lead the next generation of skill-providing platforms that will eventually spring up to cater to a growing global need. Udemy's first mover advantage, as well as its economic moat that it continues to strengthen, will be key in setting the company on the throne of this arena.

Valuation

Although Seeking Alpha's Quant scoring system gives Udemy a score of 4.88, which puts it in the 'strong buy' category, it scores a C- on its valuation-based metrics. The reason it arrives at this score is due to how much more 'expensive' UDMY appears, relative to the consumer discretionary sector:

As can be seen from the table above, UDMY holds higher multiples than the broader sector on every single metric that is listed. Its trailing PS ratio of 1.91 is almost double the consumer discretionary median ratio of 0.99. However, while the stock isn't cheap (based on a cursory glance comparing its multiples to the discretionary sector), we can see that it scores very good on growth, revisions, and its price momentum. Such a picture usually indicates that the valuation is not, in fact, inflated, but rather is inclusive of a performance premium.

In order to determine how fair UDMY's pricing is, given the relative value it provides investors with, I am going to go with my preferred scatter graph method. Through a peer set of the wider educational services industry, I will attempt to determine how the market values companies based on the forward-looking growth they offer, and draw a line of best fit accordingly, to highlight this relationship.

I have gone with EBITDA metrics in the case of UDMY, since the company is not yet profitable from an EPS basis. Also, I prefer EBITDA to revenue in the case of Udemy because the company is not singularly focused on maximizing its topline to achieve growth, but also is bent towards boosting its profitability through achieving operational efficiencies and a relentless cost-saving. For this reason, I feel that an EBITDA-based assessment is most appropriate.

For 2024, the company reported $43 million in adjusted EBITDA, at a 5.5% margin, considering its total topline of $787 million. This is quite incredible, in my opinion because just 2 years ago, for 2022, Udemy reported an adjusted EBITDA loss of almost $50 million. Even more impressive is the fact that the management expects its EBITDA, by the end of 2025, to jump to as high as $75 million to $85 million. If we assume the company meets the mid-figure of that range, it implies an expected growth rate of 86%. For context, the consumer discretionary median growth rate for EBITDA is only about 3%. You can already tell that the relatively more pricey multiples make a lot of sense for the stock.

Anyways, now that I've laid out my reasoning for my chosen method, the results of my assessment on the educational services industry can be seen in the scatter graph below:

As we can see, UDMY's EBITDA growth rate of 86% puts it far ahead of all its peers in the industry by a long shot. Moreover, the magnitude of its distance away from the trendline shows that its forward EV/EBITDA ratio of almost 14, while higher than most stocks in the peer set, actually is a bargain given the value it offers in terms of growth.

Given these value and growth dynamics, as well as its ambitious plans to further strengthen its market position through AI-powered learning experiences and a greater focus on the enterprise side of its business, I believe UDMY is poised for a massive growth trajectory. 2025 will clearly be a defining year for the company, and based on how things are looking from our current vantage point, the opportunity is simply too good to ignore.

For the reasons mentioned above, I believe UDMY is substantially undervalued, and I rate it as a strong buy.

Risk to Thesis

While I find Udemy's value proposition and growth potential highly compelling, I must point out one risk that keeps pinching at me: the delicate balance between keeping its instructors satisfied and boosting profit margins.

One reason Udemy has been able to achieve such strides in its profitability, and in its adjusted EBITDA in particular, has been by lowering the take rate applicable to instructors. It lowered the rate to 17.5% in 2024, and has mentioned that it will further be lowered to 15% by 2026. While this does deliver immediate expansion on gross profit margins, it can only be exploited to a limit, and could even undermine Udemy's long-term competitive advantage. In fact, the company plans to expand its adjusted EBITDA margin from 5.5% in 2024, to as high as 20% by 2027.

Instructors are the cornerstone of Udemy's marketplace. They not only drive its speed-to-market advantage, but also generate high-quality content that keeps the platform alive. If the compensation ends up becoming too lean for the average instructor, alternative platforms may be sought out. In fact, in the dynamic tech age of today where players like DeepSeek spring out of nowhere and disrupt established giants, Udemy would need to watch out for the emergence of a rival platform that offers a far more attractive take rate, in order to avoid a mass migration of its instructors. This could prove fatal to Udemy, in my opinion, and could even disrupt its content ecosystem, despite all the company's efforts to refine product offerings.

I understand that a balance needs to be struck between profits and incentivizing instructors. The solution will probably need to be an innovative one. Regardless, I think investors should monitor how Udemy navigates this tension.

Investor Takeaway

Udemy's growth narrative seems highly compelling to me. The company's decision to enhance focus on its lucrative enterprise business, while simultaneously equipping its platform with AI-powered learning tools and assessments, both bode very well for it. The company has recognized the massive opportunity it could tap into and is making the right moves towards this end.

In my opinion, the next 2 years will be defining for Udemy, and the company could become a massive leader in a space that will explode. Right now, it's trading at a bargain, given the massive growth potential it holds. I rate UDMY as a strong buy!
